source.cookbook: Black Rican Vegan   Fire Plant Based Recipes from
source.author: Various

ingredients.item:
COMPRA LIST
Tofu Ricotta
1 (12-oz [340-g]) block extra-firm tofu
⅓ cup (43 g) nutritional yeast
1 tbsp (15 ml) apple cider vinegar
1 tbsp (15 ml) grapeseed oil
1 tsp salt
1 tsp freshly ground black pepper
1 tsp garlic powder
1 tbsp (5.5 g) Italian seasoning
¼ cup (60 ml) nondairy milk of choice
Lasagna
1 tbsp (15 ml) olive oil
1 large white onion, diced
5 cloves garlic, minced
1 (14-oz [400-g]) package Beyond Meat sausage
1 lb (455 g) Beyond Meat ground beef
1 (6-oz [170-g]) can tomato paste
1 (28-oz [800-g]) can crushed tomatoes
1 (8-oz [225-g]) can tomato sauce
2 tbsp (25 g) raw cane sugar
1 tsp fennel seeds
2 tsp (2.5 g) fresh oregano
1 tsp minced fresh parsley
1 tsp salt
1 tsp freshly ground black pepper
1 tbsp (3 g) minced fresh basil
1 (1-lb [455-g]) box lasagna noodles
1 (8-oz [227-g]) package vegan mozzarella cheese

My dad was an unhappy man, and I don’t have the best memories of him. My mom separated from him when I was seven, and he was in and out of my life briefly until he just stopped showing up. Years passed, and in 2011, he reached out because he was in the hospital and needed heart surgery. I was happy to hear that he wanted my siblings and me to visit him, but I was also sad because of the awful memories I had of him and the fact he just disappeared from my life. Although I wanted to hate him, I just couldn’t.
My siblings and I went to Lebanon Hospital to see him for the first time in years. I couldn’t believe he and I were together again, although he wasn’t in great shape and it was a vulnerable moment for him. We all sat down, and I tried my hardest to hold back my tears. But when my sister sat down, she immediately started with the waterworks, and I couldn’t hold it in anymore and started to cry as well. He told us to stop crying and that everything was okay, but I think we were crying because, although he had been mean to us, we had missed him and still loved him.
After that day, we would visit him occasionally. I remember he texted me and told me to bring him lasagna. I told my mom and she said that was his favorite dish. I don’t think I ever brought it for him, though. I think I forgot, or we just stopped seeing him again for a while. He was too prideful to reach out to us, and I was too busy living my life. My dad stopped contacting us after some years. Then, in 2020, I was in the nail salon when the news came on and reported Kobe Bryant had died. I wasn’t a huge basketball fan, but his death hurt me, and I felt incredibly sad for a while. It impacted me and then I started to think of my dad. Weeks went by, and it was still heavy on my heart. I was in my sister’s room, and I casually asked her whether she has spoken to Dad and she replied, “No, I haven’t; I forgot where he was staying.” I told her I knew where he was, and we Googled his number the next day and called. He was at a rehab facility; when we phoned, they broke the bad news to us that he was extremely sick. No one had called us because they didn’t have our contact information. I remember feeling so angry. My sister and I called the hospital he was staying in for the doctor to tell us he had been in the ICU for 22 days and he was unconscious and not responding. My sister and I rushed home to get ready, and five minutes before we left for the hospital, they called us back to tell us he didn’t make it. I felt so much guilt and so much pain. February 14, 2020, my dad was gone. I wished for so many things to be different, but I had to stay strong for my family. As we were leaving, I’ll never forget what the nurse told me; she said, “He waited for you.” I truly believe he did, and he intentionally passed on Valentine’s Day to remind me how much he loved me, as I like to think.
My dad’s love for lasagna makes me think of Titi Mildred’s lasagna. She is always asked to make it for family gatherings or events because it is that good. Even if she doesn’t go to the event, we ask that she make the lasagna and we pick it up from her. Titi Mildred still hasn’t shared her secret with me, but she gave me a few hints about what she does, so I ran with that information and re-created my best vegan version of it. It still doesn’t quite compare, but I think I am close. The lasagna is creamy, savory, saucy and herby. Every bite is an explosion of flavor. I wish I could have brought my dad a piece of Titi’s lasagna. He would have loved it just as much as I do.

COMPRA LIST

1 (12-oz [340-g]) block extra-firm tofu
⅓ cup (43 g) nutritional yeast

1 tsp garlic powder

1 tbsp (15 ml) olive oil
1 large white onion, diced
5 cloves garlic, minced

1 (6-oz [170-g]) can tomato paste
1 (28-oz [800-g]) can crushed tomatoes
1 (8-oz [225-g]) can tomato sauce

1 tbsp (3 g) minced fresh basil

1 (8-oz [227-g]) package vegan mozzarella cheese

ingredients.item:
COCINANDO
Make the Tofu Ricotta
Remove the tofu from the package and, using two paper towels, gently squeeze the extra water from the tofu block. Break up the tofu, place it in a blender and pulse about three times until you get big crumbles.
Add your nutritional yeast, apple cider vinegar, grapeseed oil, salt, pepper, garlic powder, Italian seasoning and nondairy milk to the blender. Blend everything together until smooth, then set aside.
The top photo is my Titi Mildred. The bottom is my mom, my sister and my dad, 1990.
Make the Lasagna
In a pot over medium heat, heat your olive oil, then add your white onion and garlic and cook until tender, about 4 minutes.
Add your Beyond Meat sausage and beef and break them down until you get crumbles. Mix in your tomato paste, stirring until the sauce turns red and there are no tomato paste clumps left. Gradually add the crushed tomato and tomato sauce while stirring.
Follow that with the cane sugar, fennel seeds, oregano, parsley, salt, pepper and basil, and combine thoroughly. Lower the heat to low and let the sauce simmer for 15 minutes for the flavors to settle in.
In a large baking dish, lay all the lasagna noodles on the bottom of the dish. Submerge the noodles in hot water and let them sit for about 30 minutes. (Hot water from the tap is fine for this—it doesn’t need to be boiling.) This softens the noodles a bit before assembling and baking. It also prevents them from absorbing all the sauce.
Remove the water and noodles from the dish, draining the noodles of the water and placing them on a plate. Preheat the oven to 400°F (200°C).
Spread ¼ cup (60 ml) of meat sauce on the bottom of the baking dish, covering the bottom completely. Lay down four to five lasagna noodles, covering the layer of meat sauce evenly. Spread a layer of tofu ricotta on top of the noodles, then spread another layer of meat sauce and cover with another layer of lasagna noodles.
Repeat by spreading a layer of tofu ricotta on top of the noodles, then a layer of meat sauce. Top the meat sauce with a handful of the vegan mozzarella cheese all over and cover with another layer of lasagna noodles. Repeat one more time. When you get to the very top, add some meat sauce, a few dollops of tofu ricotta and another handful of the mozzarella.
Cover the baking dish with foil and bake for 35 minutes. Then, remove the foil and bake for an additional 10 minutes to brown up the cheese a bit.
Take the lasagna out the oven and get ready to serve. ¡Buen provecho!
